title: Catechist Guide — The Our Father
html:
Catechist Guide

For preparing and leading the class. For exclusive use of the catechist.

Class Summary

Estimated Duration: 60 minutes

Central Theme: The Our Father as the summary of the Gospel: the prayer Jesus taught us, where we learn to call God "Abba."

Objectives:

  • Understand the unique place of the Our Father in Christian prayer.
  • Know the structure of the prayer: invocation and seven petitions.
  • Discover the meaning of Abba and filial trust.
  • Appreciate the communal dimension of "Our Father."
  • Understand "who art in heaven" as God's majesty, not a place.
  • Experience the Our Father in a conscious and renewed way.

Key Verse: "Lord, teach us to pray" (Lk 11:1)

Catechism References: CCC 2759-2802

Suggested Class Structure

Section Duration Catechist Actions
1. Big Question 8 min Ask: "If God said to you 'Call me Dad,' how would you feel?" Narrate the scene of the disciples asking Jesus to teach them to pray. Introduce Tertullian and the concept of the Our Father as summary of the Gospel.
2. What Is the Our Father? 12 min Explain the unique place of this prayer in the Church. Note that the Catechism devotes all of Part Four, over a hundred paragraphs, to commenting on it. Present the structure: invocation + 7 petitions (3 about God, 4 about us). Note the two biblical versions: Matthew and Luke.
3. "Our Father": Abba 10 min Explain the meaning of Abba in Aramaic. Cite Romans 8:15 and Galatians 4:6. Emphasize the communal dimension of "our": it is not my Father, but our Father. St. John Chrysostom. Connect with human experience of fatherhood, healing wounds.
4. "Who Art in Heaven" 8 min Dispel the misunderstanding: "heaven is not a place, but a way of being of God." Discuss divine transcendence and majesty. Heaven as goal, as horizon of hope.
5. The Prayer of the New Covenant 5 min Contrast the Law of Moses with the prayer of Jesus. Explain that it can only be fully prayed with the Holy Spirit. Mention Fulton Sheen.
6. For Reflection 7 min Exercise: pray the Our Father aloud, very slowly, word by word. Then share in pairs which word "strikes" today. Some share with the whole group.
7. Closing: Key Idea + Prayer 5 min Key Idea. Pray the Our Father together aloud, slowly and consciously. Ask that they do not recite it mechanically this week.
8. Activity for the Week 5 min Assign one phrase of the Our Father each day to meditate on for five minutes. Encourage writing reflections. Distribute the workbook.

Notes for the Catechist

The Our Father on Autopilot

This is the great difficulty: we have all said the Our Father thousands of times and we risk praying it without thinking. The catechist's challenge is to help students rediscover it as if for the first time. Have them pray it very slowly. If possible, have them read it rather than recite it from memory. Memory is sometimes the enemy of attention.

The Image of God as Father

Some people have deep wounds with their earthly father: abandonment, abuse, absence, coldness. If someone shares something like this, do not minimize it or resolve it theologically with an argument. Listen with compassion. God is Father, but he is not like human fathers. Sometimes a long process of healing is necessary before one can say "Our Father" without pain. This is normal and deserves respect.

"Our" Challenges Individualism

We live in a highly individualistic culture. Saying "Our Father" is a countercultural act. I am not alone before God: I am part of a family. The Our Father compels me to look at others and recognize them as brothers and sisters. This is especially important in settings where there are divisions or conflicts in the parish community or in the catechism class.

Heaven Is Not a Place

Many people, especially children, think God is literally in the clouds. It is important to clarify that this is symbolic language to express God's transcendence, without falling into childish literalism or vague spiritualism. God is everywhere, but saying "who art in heaven" reminds us that we do not possess him, we do not control him, we do not reduce him to our categories.

Adaptation by Age

🌱 Seed (children 7-12)

Explain that the Our Father is the prayer Jesus taught us personally. Ask: "What do you call your dad when you love him very much?" Jesus taught us to call God "Dad" in a special way (Abba). Activity: have each child draw what they imagine when they say "Our Father who art in heaven" (they will likely draw clouds and an old man; use this to explain that it is not a place, but that God is so great he cannot fit in any drawing).

🌿 Sprouts (youth 13-17)

Discuss filial identity. Adolescents are forming their identity, often in conflict with their parents. The good news of the Our Father is: you have a Father who will not fail you. Ask: "If you could talk to God the way you talk to your best friend, what would change?" Mention how the Our Father is countercultural: in a world of competition, it tells us we are brothers and sisters ("our").

🪴 Root (adults new to the faith)

Many adults new to the faith come from traditions where prayer is totally improvised. Show the beauty of having words given by Jesus himself. It is not rigidity; it is school. Explain the structure of the prayer and how each petition balances the others. Invite them to use the Our Father as a skeleton for their personal prayer.

🌳 Tree (formed adults)

Deepen the theology of the Our Father within Part Four of the Catechism. Connect with the patristic tradition: Tertullian, St. Cyprian, and St. Augustine wrote monumental commentaries on the Our Father. Mention the Gregorian chant of the Pater Noster. Explore how the Our Father is the Trinitarian prayer par excellence: to the Father, through the Son, in the Holy Spirit.

Supplementary Resources

  • YouCat: Questions 493-499 and 501-510 (on the Our Father).
  • St. Cyprian of Carthage: "On the Lord's Prayer" — fundamental patristic treatise from the third century.
  • Fulton Sheen: Life of Christ — contains an accessible and profound commentary on the Our Father.
  • Raniero Cantalamessa: The Our Father — reflections from the Preacher of the Papal Household.
  • Simone Weil: "On the Our Father" — a philosophical reading from the perspective of conversion to Christianity.
  • Music: Pater Noster in Gregorian chant (Schola Cantorum). Also modern versions: Taizé and Marco Frisina.
  • Art: "Jesus Teaching the Our Father" by James Tissot (19th century).

Difficult Questions

"I can't say 'Father' because my father abandoned me. How do I resolve this?"

With patience and compassion. The image of God the Father can heal the wound of human fatherhood, but not overnight. God does not take offense if today you struggle to call him "Father." He is the Father who waits, like the one in the parable of the prodigal son. The important thing is not to close yourself off. Pray the Our Father even if it hurts, even if only the first word. In time, grace transforms the wound into a path of healing.

"If God is Father of everyone, can non-Christians also pray the Our Father?"

The Church teaches that God loves everyone and wills the salvation of all. But the Our Father, in its fullness, is a prayer of the baptized: it presupposes faith in Jesus Christ as the Son of God. That said, nothing prevents someone who sincerely seeks God from praying the Our Father; grace works beyond the visible limits of the Church. What matters is the attitude of the heart.

"Why are there two versions of the Our Father in the Bible?"

Each evangelist adapted Jesus's teachings according to his audience and theological purpose. Matthew wrote for Christians of Jewish origin and includes seven petitions (the number of fullness). Luke wrote for Christians of pagan origin and his version is briefer. The Church adopted Matthew's version for the liturgy because it is the most complete. Both are authentic teachings of Jesus.

"Is the Our Father a magical prayer?"

No. Christian prayer is not magical. It is not a formula to force God to do what we want. It is a dialogue of love and trust. The Our Father works not because of the words themselves but because it opens us to relationship with the Father as Jesus lived it. Praying it with faith transforms us from within, and that transformation is the true answer to our prayer.

Catechist's Prayer

Our Father, who art in heaven,
You who have called me to teach others what you taught me first,
I ask that my words not be my own,
but an echo of those your Son left us.
May whoever hears me not see me,
but glimpse your face as Father.
And may I, in teaching to pray,
also learn to do so.
Through Jesus Christ, your Son, in the Holy Spirit. Amen.

Print Catechist Guide
